perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the party disclosing Confidential Information, as defined below, is the “ Disclosing Party ” and the party receiving the Confidential Information is the “ Recipient ”.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the party disclosing Confidential Information, as defined below, is the “ Disclosing Party ”. The party receiving the Confidential Information is the “ Recipient ”, unless the Recipient is a direct competitor of the Disclosing Party, in which case they are also considered a Disclosing Party for liability purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The change to the definition of 'Recipient' introduces an in-text contradiction. Section 1 defines Recipient as the party receiving confidential information, but this modification adds a caveat where a Recipient can also be a Disclosing Party under certain circumstances. This conflicts with the rest of the agreement, which is based on the original definitions where the roles are distinct. The contradiction specifically impacts sections outlining the Recipient's responsibilities and liabilities, as now those could also apply to direct competitors who were initially defined as Recipients.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Confidential Information will be used solely for the purposes of evaluating the Proposed Transaction, provided, however, that the Recipient may disclose any Confidential Information to its Representatives who need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating the Proposed Transaction. Prior to disclosing the Confidential Information to any Representative, however, the Recipient shall inform the Representative of the confidential nature of such information and undertake reasonable efforts to cause them to treat such information on a confidential basis. The Recipient shall be responsible for the breach of this Agreement by its Representatives that have not separately signed a non-disclosure agreement with the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives, and shall take all reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Information. The Disclosing Party and its Representatives shall otherwise have no recourse against any of the Recipient’s Representatives that have not separately signed a non-disclosure agreement with the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Recipient' to include direct competitors of the Disclosing Party under certain liability circumstances. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which outlines the Recipient's responsibilities regarding Confidential Information and its Representatives. The contradicted text assumes a clear distinction between Disclosing Party and Recipient, with the Recipient being responsible for breaches. However, the modified text blurs this distinction by making a direct competitor also a Disclosing Party in some instances. This creates a strong in-text contradiction regarding who bears responsibility for unauthorized disclosures, fitting the definition of inconsistencies leading to in-text contradictions.

perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information will be used solely for the purposes of evaluating the Proposed Transaction, provided, however, that the Recipient may disclose any Confidential Information to its Representatives who need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating the Proposed Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information will be used solely for the purposes of evaluating the Proposed Transaction, unless otherwise stated in a separate agreement. The Recipient may not disclose any Confidential Information to its Representatives without prior written consent from the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 2 introduces an in-text contradiction by changing the conditions under which Confidential Information can be used and disclosed. The original text allows disclosure to Representatives for evaluation, but the revised text requires separate agreement for any other use, contradicting the stated allowance for Representatives. This change creates uncertainty about the Recipient's ability to utilize the information as initially intended, and impacts sections concerning liability and permitted use of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nothing in this Agreement shall prohibit or limit the Recipient’s use of information (including, but not limited to, ideas, concepts, know-how, techniques, and methodologies) (i) that was known to Recipient or any of its Representatives before disclosure by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives, (ii) that was or becomes available to the Recipient or any of its Representatives from a source other than the Disclosing Party or its Representatives, provided that such source is not known by the Recipient to be prohibited from disclosing such information by a contractual, legal, or fiduciary obligation to the Disclosing Party or its Representatives, (iii) which is or becomes publicly available other than as a result of a disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or (iv) which is independently developed by Recipient or any of its Representatives without reference to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction by altering the conditions for using and disclosing Confidential Information. Originally, disclosure to Representatives for evaluating the Proposed Transaction was allowed. The revised text requires prior written consent for any disclosure to Representatives, directly conflicting with the contradicted text, which allows the Recipient’s use of information known to its Representatives before disclosure. This creates a clear and direct conflict, making it difficult to determine if the Recipient can use information already known to its Representatives, thus meeting the definition of inconsistencies.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, each party agrees that without the prior written consent of the other, it will not, and it will direct its Representatives not to, disclose to any person, other than its Representatives, either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Proposed Transaction between IDT and PLX or any of the terms and conditions with respect to the Proposed Transaction and no Confidential Information will be provided to such persons.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, each party agrees that without the prior written consent of the other, it will not, and it will direct its Representatives to disclose to any person the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Proposed Transaction between IDT and PLX or some of the terms and conditions with respect to the Proposed Transaction and Confidential Information will be provided to such persons for the purpose of gauging market feedback.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 3 introduces an in-text contradiction by changing Representatives direction on disclosure on Proposed Transaction. The original text direct Representatives to not disclose, but the modified text instructs them to disclose to any person the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Proposed Transaction for the purpose of gauging market feedback. This directly conflicts with the core purpose of the NDA, which is to keep information confidential. The change directly impacts Section 13, which provides that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a party or its Representatives and that, in addition to all other available remedies, either party may be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties agree that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a party or its Representatives and that, in addition to all other available remedies, either party may be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text instructs Representatives to disclose information, directly contradicting the original intent of Section 3 and the expectation of confidentiality implied in Section 13. Section 13 provides that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a party or its Representatives. The changed text directs Representatives to disclose information about the Proposed Transaction, this directly conflicts with the original text of the agreement. The instruction to disclose information fundamentally undermines the confidentiality intended by the original agreement and creates an in-text contradiction with Section 13, therefore, the answer is YES.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall at all times remain, as between the parties, the property of both the Disclosing Party and the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 5 introduces an in-text contradiction by changing the ownership of Confidential Information. The original text specifies it remains the property of the Disclosing Party, but the altered text states it is owned by both parties. This affects sections concerning the rights to use, return, or destroy the information, creating ambiguity about who has ultimate control. The change directly contradicts Section 6, which is related to Return of Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Recipient shall promptly destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all written Confidential Information (whether prepared by the Disclosing party or its Representatives) and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written materials except that (i) one copy of each electronic version of such materials may be retained by Recipient and its Representatives in their archives pursuant to such party’s standard data backup and disaster recovery plans and (ii) Recipient’s outside legal counsel and accountants may retain one hard copy of such materials in its archives solely for regulatory or corporate records retention policy compliance and for dispute resolution; provided, however, that any Confidential Information contained in such format may not be used for any other purpose and that all obligations contained herein shall, with respect to any Confidential Information so retained, survive any termination of this Agreement. Return and/or destruction of Confidential Information and copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof and documents, memoranda, notes, and other writings to the extent that they contain Confidential Information shall be certified in writing to the Disclosing Party by an authorized officer of the Recipient supervising such destruction upon the Disclosing Party’s written request.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 5, altering the ownership of Confidential Information from the Disclosing Party to both the Disclosing Party and the Recipient. This directly contradicts Section 6, which grants the Disclosing Party the right to request the return or destruction of all Confidential Information. If both parties own the information, the Disclosing Party's ability to demand its return or destruction is undermined, creating a clear in-text contradiction as it creates ambiguity over the rights of each party regarding the confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that unless and until a definitive written agreement or other binding agreement between the parties with respect to a relationship of the type contemplated by the Proposed Transaction has been executed and delivered by each party hereto, no party will be under any obligation of any kind whatsoever with respect to such a relationship by virtue of this or any written or oral expression concerning such a transaction by any of its Representatives except, in the case of this Agreement, for the matters specifically agreed to herein.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that unless and until a definitive written agreement or other binding agreement between the parties with respect to a relationship of the type contemplated by the Proposed Transaction has been executed and delivered by each party hereto, each party will be under obligation of good faith with respect to such a relationship by virtue of this or any written or oral expression concerning such a transaction by any of its Representatives including matters not specifically agreed to herein.
perturbation.explanation: This modification to Section 14 introduces an in-text contradiction by adding an obligation of good faith. The original text states that no party will be under any obligation unless a definitive agreement is reached, but the modified text introduces an obligation of good faith, even for matters not explicitly agreed upon. This conflicts with the intended limitations on liability and obligations. This impacts the interpretation of the entire agreement, particularly concerning potential claims for breach of good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Although each party will endeavor to include in the Confidential Information items known to it which it believes to be relevant for the purpose of the other party’s investigation and analysis of the Proposed Transaction, each party understands and agrees that neither the other party nor any of its Representatives have made or make any representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information. In particular, and without limitation of the foregoing, each party understands that any projected or forecasted financial, Page 3 of 5 March 31, 2012 operating, performance, strategy or other information reflects merely the judgment of management of such party at the time of the preparation of such information, and is based upon a number of factors and circumstances beyond the control of the party and its management. Accordingly, there can be no assurance that actual results or performance will be in line with any such projections or forecasts. Each party agrees that, except as otherwise specifically agreed to in a definitive written transaction agreement or other binding agreement, neither party nor any of its Representatives shall have any liability to the other party or any of its affiliates or Representatives arising out of or resulting from the use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 14 states that no party is under any obligation unless a definitive agreement is reached. The changed text introduces an obligation of good faith even for matters not explicitly agreed upon. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which states that neither party shall have any liability to the other party arising out of the use of confidential information, "except as otherwise specifically agreed to in a definitive written transaction agreement". By introducing an obligation of good faith before a definitive agreement, the changed text creates a liability that the original agreement sought to avoid, resulting in a strong in-text contradiction according to the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be executed in separate counterparts, each of which counterparts shall be deemed an original and all of which counterparts shall together constitute one and the Page 4 of 5 March 31, 2012 same agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be executed in separate counterparts, each of which counterparts shall be deemed a preliminary agreement and all of which counterparts shall together constitute one and the Page 4 of 5 March 31, 2012 same agreement until a complete draft is agreed upon.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 16 introduces an in-text contradiction by altering the status of the signed counterparts. The original text defines each counterpart as an original, legally binding agreement. The changed text defines them as preliminary agreements until a final draft is agreed upon, which would negate the legal force of the initial signing. This conflicts with sections related to enforcement and remedies, because the agreement is no longer considered fully binding. This impacts Section 15, which outlines that the agreement sets forth the entire agreement and understanding of the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, arrangements, understandings, promises and assurances relating thereto.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire agreement and understanding of the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, arrangements, understandings, promises and assurances relating thereto. This Agreement is for the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective advisors, directors, officers, employees, shareholders, owners, affiliates, representatives and agents, and shall be governed by, construed, interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without giving effect to principles of conflict of laws which would result in the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of the agreement counterparts from 'original' to 'preliminary'. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the agreement is the 'entire agreement and understanding' and supersedes all prior agreements. If the counterparts are preliminary, the agreement cannot be the 'entire agreement,' leading to a strong in-text contradiction because the legal force of the agreement is negated until a final draft is agreed upon. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition, specifically where 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting...obligations'.
